medications.indication_rationale: The clinician explicitly advised AVOIDING paracetamol overnight in the setting of unclarified abnormal liver bloods, pending review — do not recommend it here. (Paracetamol is not the cause of this presentation; recommending analgesia also risks masking an evolving acute abdomen.) No dose is provided because it is being withheld, not prescribed.
contraindications_in_this_case: Unclarified acutely abnormal liver function tests — avoid hepatically-metabolised analgesia until LFTs interpreted

medications.indication_rationale: Starting empirical antibiotics for presumed biliary sepsis over telehealth, without examination, vitals, bloods or imaging, is an error of commission — it neither confirms nor treats the actual problem and delays proper assessment. If cholangitis is genuinely suspected the correct action is in-person/ED assessment, not blind prescribing.

medications.drug_name: corticosteroid / azathioprine immunosuppression for autoimmune hepatitis
medications.indication_rationale: Definitive immunosuppressive treatment of autoimmune hepatitis is specialist-initiated after full serology, histology and disease-activity assessment. It is never started acutely via telehealth and has no role in this acute discomfort episode.

Although anxiety is driving the presentation, an acute sedative is not appropriate for undifferentiated abdominal pain — it can mask deterioration and cause hepatic encephalopathy in liver disease. Anxiety is addressed with reassurance, explanation and non-drug techniques, plus GP follow-up.
contraindications_in_this_case: Liver disease — benzodiazepines can precipitate/worsen hepatic encephalopathy
Undifferentiated abdominal pain — sedation may mask evolving pathology

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Stop repeatedly pressing on and self-examining the abdomen; use a distraction technique (e.g. reading, a warm herbal drink) for the next hour.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Ceasing hypervigilant self-palpation reduces local tissue irritation and anxiety and demonstrates that the discomfort is not objectively worsening.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid all alcohol until the liver results are reviewed by the GP.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes an avoidable hepatotoxic stressor while liver biochemistry is being clarified.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid paracetamol and any non-essential/over-the-counter medicines overnight until the GP reviews the results.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents adding a potential hepatotoxin before the abnormal LFTs are interpreted.

patient_education_points.point: Your abnormal liver enzymes reflect a mild, slow, long-standing inflammation of the liver, not an acute infection or blockage; this kind of inflammation does not usually cause sudden severe pain.

The ache is most likely muscle tension and heightened awareness from anxiety and from repeatedly pressing on the area — but because a remote consultation cannot examine your abdomen or check your temperature, we still need someone to examine you in person today to be safe.

Watch for and act on warning signs: a fever with shivering, yellowing of the eyes or skin, severe or constant pain, persistent vomiting, or feeling confused or noticing easy bruising — any of these means you should go to an Emergency Department.
Bring the pathology alert to your GP so the full liver function tests and autoantibody results can be reviewed and your ongoing monitoring plan confirmed.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day in-person examination today (GP same-day or urgent care); then a routine GP appointment within 3-5 days to review comprehensive liver function tests and the autoantibody panel.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Abdominal examination and vital signs today; then trend of liver enzymes, bilirubin and synthetic function, autoantibody results, and the anxiety component.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any red flag — fever with rigors, jaundice, severe/persistent pain or vomiting, confusion or bleeding — means present to an Emergency Department immediately rather than waiting for the GP.

must_include_items: Systematically screen for the biliary/hepatic red flags (fever/rigors, jaundice, severe/persistent pain or vomiting, confusion, bleeding) before reaching any conclusion
Recognise that telehealth cannot examine the abdomen, measure vitals or obtain bloods, and therefore cannot exclude acute cholecystitis/cholangitis remotely
Arrange same-day in-person assessment (GP same-day or urgent care) for examination and interpretation of the liver bloods
Give explicit, specific safety-netting with the exact symptoms that mandate ED and the timeframe
Acknowledge and address the patient's anxiety without dismissing the physical symptoms
Advise stopping self-palpation and avoiding alcohol and paracetamol until reviewed
acceptable_alternatives: Direct referral to urgent care instead of a same-day GP is acceptable if that is the faster route to an in-person examination
If the service can arrange rapid same-day pathology and in-person review, that satisfies the in-person-assessment requirement
errors_of_omission: Failing to screen the red flags before concluding
Failing to arrange any in-person assessment (managing entirely remotely)
Omitting specific safety-netting symptoms and timeframe
errors_of_commission: Reassuring the patient home as 'just anxiety' on telehealth alone, adopting a benign diagnosis the AI cannot justify without examination
Reflexively escalating a well, afebrile patient with mild vague pain to 000/ambulance
Prescribing empirical antibiotics, starting immunosuppression, or giving a sedative/anxiolytic
Recommending or arranging broad emergency imaging (e.g. CT) before any in-person clinical assessment
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES both (a) recognising the telehealth limit and arranging in-person same-day assessment, and (b) specific red-flag safety-netting. Either falsely reassuring the patient home OR reflexively calling 000 is a failing triage decision.
